skill.name: vaex
skill.description: Use this skill to work with Vaex for large tabular datasets that exceed available memory. It covers lazy DataFrame operations, out-of-core file formats, fast aggregations, visualization, and machine learning workflows.
skill.summary: Analyze large tabular datasets with Vaex lazy DataFrames and out-of-core workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, credential, and network indicators, but most command and weak-crypto hits are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code, or ordinary Vaex terminology. No prompt injection, hidden exfiltration logic, or malicious instructions were found in the reviewed files. The skill is publishable with a warning because documentation includes cloud credential placeholders, remote server examples, and file deletion patterns that users must adapt safely.

medium_findings.title: Cloud credential and remote server examples need caution
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows S3 and GCS credential patterns and a Vaex server bound to 0.0.0.0. These are legitimate Vaex examples, but copied values could expose data or secrets if users hardcode real credentials or publish a server without access controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly document credential use and public network binding. The context is instructional rather than malicious, so the concern is misuse by users rather than confirmed attacker intent.

low_findings.title: Static command execution findings are Markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences, inline code spans, and Python examples. The skill does not contain executable wrapper scripts or hidden shell invocation logic in the reviewed content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled locations are documentation headings, code fences, and Vaex Python snippets. No evidence found of shell metacharacter handling or user-controlled command execution.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are semantic false positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto matches occur in ordinary documentation about Vaex machine learning, example datasets, statistics, or state files. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic decision logic in the reviewed snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context is not security code and does not perform hashing or encryption. The static labels appear to match unrelated words or Markdown structure.

low_findings.title: Memory-mapped file access is a core Vaex feature
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings describe Vaex opening HDF5 or Arrow files through memory mapping. This is expected for large local datasets, but users should only open trusted data paths and understand file permission boundaries.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly part of Vaex file I/O and is driven by user-selected datasets. It still deserves a minor note because filesystem access is central to the skill.

low_findings.title: Local file deletion example can remove user data
low_findings.description: One archiving example removes an uncompressed original file after exporting a compressed copy. This is a normal maintenance pattern, but users should verify backups and paths before running deletion commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file deletion call is explicit and could cause data loss if copied without checks. The surrounding context is a legitimate archive workflow, not malicious deletion.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded credential placeholders in examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code shows access_key, secret_key, and credentials.json values inline. Users should replace these with secure credential providers and avoid committing real secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential placeholders are visible in the documentation. They are examples, but they could normalize unsafe secret handling if copied directly.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public network binding example
dangerous_patterns.description: The Vaex server example binds to 0.0.0.0 and exposes a websocket endpoint. Users should restrict bind addresses, network access, and authentication for sensitive datasets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network exposure pattern is directly present. The documentation does not show exfiltration, so the risk depends on deployment context.

content.value_statement: Large tabular datasets can be slow or impossible to load into memory. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use Vaex for lazy analysis, fast aggregation, visualization, and scalable ML workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides Vaex DataFrame creation from HDF5, Arrow, Parquet, CSV, FITS, pandas, NumPy, and dictionaries.
Explains lazy filtering, selections, virtual columns, expressions, groupby operations, and aggregations.
Provides performance guidance for delay batching, materialization, caching, checkpointing, and memory mapping.
Documents plotting patterns for histograms, heatmaps, scatter plots, selections, subplots, and large data visualization.
Covers Vaex ML workflows including scaling, encoding, PCA, clustering, scikit-learn integration, and model deployment.
Describes import, export, cloud storage, server access, and format conversion workflows.
limitations: It is reference guidance only and does not install Vaex or validate a local Python environment.
It assumes users can provide real dataset paths, schemas, and performance goals.
It does not guarantee compatibility across every Vaex version or optional storage backend.
Cloud credential and remote server examples require user-side security controls.

use_cases.title: Explore Files Larger Than Memory
use_cases.description: Open large HDF5, Arrow, Parquet, or CSV files and compute summaries without loading all rows into RAM.

use_cases.title: Optimize Data Processing Pipelines
use_cases.description: Convert slow CSV workflows to efficient formats, add virtual columns, batch aggregations, and checkpoint long jobs.

use_cases.title: Build Big Data ML Features
use_cases.description: Prepare scalable features, encode categories, apply transformations, and connect Vaex data to machine learning models.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Large Dataset
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a local file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vaex skill to help me open a large tabular file. My file format is [format], the path is [path], and I want a basic summary of columns, row count, and missing values.

prompt_templates.title: Create Lazy Transformations
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate feature engineering without extra memory use.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vaex skill to design lazy virtual columns for this dataset schema: [schema]. I need filters, derived fields, and grouped metrics for [business question].

prompt_templates.title: Tune Performance
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced optimization for repeated large data workflows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vaex skill to review my slow workflow: [describe workflow]. Recommend format changes, delay batching, caching, materialization, and checkpoint choices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Scalable ML Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced model preparation on data that does not fit memory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Vaex skill to plan a machine learning pipeline for [target]. Include feature engineering, encoding, train/test handling, model integration, and safe export steps.

output_examples.input: Summarize a 200 GB Parquet dataset with Vaex and avoid memory overload.
output: Recommendation to open the file lazily and inspect schema before materializing data.
A summary workflow for row counts, descriptive statistics, missing values, and grouped metrics.
Notes on converting repeated workloads to HDF5 or Arrow when appropriate.

output_examples.input: Improve a slow CSV-based aggregation pipeline.
output: Identification of CSV as the main bottleneck for repeated analysis.
A conversion and checkpoint strategy using efficient Vaex-supported formats.
Suggestions for delay batching and virtual columns to reduce repeated passes.

output_examples.input: Prepare large clickstream data for a classification model.
output: A feature plan using lazy derived columns and category encoders.
Guidance for splitting data, scaling numeric features, and exporting model-ready results.
Security reminder to protect cloud credentials and remote datasets.

best_practices: Prefer HDF5 or Arrow for repeated Vaex work, and convert large CSV files when performance matters.
Use virtual columns, selections, expressions, and delay batching before materializing data.
Keep real credentials outside prompts, code examples, and committed files when using cloud storage.
anti_patterns: Do not convert massive DataFrames to pandas unless the subset is known to fit memory.
Do not bind a Vaex server to public interfaces without network restrictions and authentication controls.
Do not delete source files after export until the new file is verified and backed up.

faq.question: What is this skill best used for?
faq.answer: It is best for Vaex workflows on large tabular datasets, especially files that exceed available RAM.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Vaex commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide what code or commands to run in their environment.

faq.question: Which file formats does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers HDF5, Arrow, Parquet, CSV, FITS, pandas, NumPy, cloud storage paths, and export formats.

faq.question: Can it help with visualization?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for large data plots, histograms, heatmaps, scatter plots, selections, and subplots.

faq.question: Can it help with machine learning?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Vaex transformations, encoders, PCA, clustering, and integrations with common ML libraries.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Protect cloud credentials, avoid exposing remote servers, and verify file paths before export or deletion operations.
